coredata.dc:title: Aptamer induced assembly of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots on gold nanoparticles for sensitive detection of AFB1
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Bin
Chen, Yanfen
Wu, Yuanya
Weng, Bo
Liu, Yingshuai
Lu, Zhisong
Li, Chang Ming
Yu, Cong
coredata.dc:description: Novel fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N,C-dots) were synthesized and assembled on aptamer modified gold nanoparticles (Aptamer/AuNPs) for the super sensitive detection of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1). Positively charged N,C-dots were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of pancreatin. The prepared N,C-dots were assembled on aptamer/AuNPs by electrostatic interactions. The fluorescence of the N,C-dots was efficiently quenched. When AFB1 was added to the assay solution, specific interactions between AFB1 and the aptamer caused release of the N,C-dots. The fluorescence of the N,C-dots recovered and the intensity increase could be used to calculate the amount of AFB1 added. The assay exhibits super-high sensitivity with a detection limit of 5pg/mL (16pM) and a wide range of linear response of 5pg/mL to 2.00ng/mL. A novel aptasensor is thus successfully constructed, it provides an efficient way for sensitive AFB1 sensing as well as a new technique for aptamer based novel sensor construction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aptamer
AuNPs
AFB1
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N,C-dots and AuNPs
Effect of C-dots on fluorescence recovery
Determination of AFB1 in the peanut and corn samples
The application of the proposed method for the detection of OTA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Basic Research Project (973 Program, Nos. 2013CB127804, 2011CB911002 ), PhD start-up grants SWU113112, SWU113111 from Southwest University , and ACBM 2014004 from Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), Wuhan University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) and XPS spectrum (B) of the as-prepared N,C-dots.
TEM of AuNPs (A), N,C-dots/Aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposite (B). HRTEM images of the AuNPs (C) and N,C-dots/Aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposite (D).

Emission spectra of the C-dots (a), Aptamer/AuNPs+C-dots (b), and C-dots/Aptamer/AuNPs+AFB1 (c) (The C-dots in A, B, C, and D, were N,C-dots, C-dots-2, C-dots-3, and C-dots-4, respectively; the concentrations of the C-dots and AFB1 were 3µg/mL and 4ng/mL, respectively. The excitation and emission wavelength were 370nm and 445nm, respectively.)

Emission spectrum of the N,C-dots/aptamer/AuNPs upon the addition of AFB1 (A) in different concentrations; linear calibration curve of the aptasensor for AFB1 (B) detection. (The excitation and emission wavelength were 370nm and 445nm, respectively.)

A. Selectivity of the assay. (Other mycotoxins include FB1, DON, ZEN, OTA, AFB2 and AFG1. The concentration of N,C-dots and AFB1 was 2.8μg/mL and 2.4ng/mL, respectively. The other mycotoxins were all at 10ng/mL). B. Control experiments: fluorescence spectra of the N,C-dots (a), AuNPs+N,C-dots (b), AuNPs+N,C-dots+AFB1 (c), AuNPs/r-DNA/ N,C-dots (d), and AuNPs/r-DNA/N,C-dots+AFB1 (e). (The excitation and emission wavelength were 370nm and 445nm, respectively.)

Emission spectrum of the N,C-dots/aptamer/AuNPs upon the addition of OTA (A) in different concentrations; linear calibration curve of the aptasensor for OTA (B) detection. (The excitation and emission wavelength were 370nm and 445nm, respectively.)

head.ce:title: Aptamer induced assembly of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots on gold nanoparticles for sensitive detection of AFB1

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), Wuhan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N,C-dots) were synthesized and assembled on aptamer modified gold nanoparticles (Aptamer/AuNPs) for the super sensitive detection of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1). Positively charged N,C-dots were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of pancreatin. The prepared N,C-dots were assembled on aptamer/AuNPs by electrostatic interactions. The fluorescence of the N,C-dots was efficiently quenched. When AFB1 was added to the assay solution, specific interactions between AFB1 and the aptamer caused release of the N,C-dots. The fluorescence of the N,C-dots recovered and the intensity increase could be used to calculate the amount of AFB1 added. The assay exhibits super-high sensitivity with a detection limit of 5pg/mL (16pM) and a wide range of linear response of 5pg/mL to 2.00ng/mL. A novel aptasensor is thus successfully constructed, it provides an efficient way for sensitive AFB1 sensing as well as a new technique for aptamer based novel sensor construction.

ce:section.ce:para: As a class of novel fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (C-dots) have attracted tremendous attentions in recent years owing to their captivating properties such as excellent photostability, favorable biocompatibility, low toxicity, and good water solubility (Lim et al., 2015; Baker and Baker, 2010). Tremendous efforts have been made to develop high yield C-dots synthetic methods. These methods can be generally classified as the top-down methods (direct cutting or exfoliation of C-dots from large carbon materials such as graphite, carbon nanotubes, activate carbon, or carbon fiber) (Ponomarenko et al., 2008; Qian et al., 2015; Qiao et al. 2010; Peng et al., 2012), and the bottom-up methods (treatment of organic precursors such as citric acid, glucose, polymer, or some natural materials chemically or physically) (Dong et al., 2012; Hu et al., 2015; Hsu and Chang, 2012; Mehta et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2012). Although C-dots prepared from the top-down methods contain oxygen-containing functional groups and with low fluorescence quantum yields (<5%), their photophysical performance can be improved by appropriate surface functionalization or passivation (Tetsuka et al., 2012). By contrast, C-dots obtained by the bottom-up methods usually possess more organic functional groups and with higher fluorescence intensity. The nitrogen-doped C-dots (N,C-dots) demonstrate especially rich photophysical properties due to the existence of N-containing functional groups on the surface of the C-dots (Deng et al., 2015; Dong et al., 2013; Simões et al., 2014).

In order to expand the areas of application, investigations on C-dots surface modification with a variety of functionalities have been carried out by many researchers (Lim et al., 2015; Tetsuka et al., 2012). These organic groups show great influence on the surface properties of the C-dots. Common C-dots display negative surface potential due to the existence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, or epoxy functional groups on the surface. Nitrogen-doped C-dots (N,C-dots) usually exhibit positive surface potential originated from nitrogenous organic precursors. The amino groups on the surface are essential to the properties of the N,C-dots, which can not only improve their fluorescence but also facilitate subsequent modification and application. For example, (Yu et al., 2013) synthesized amino-coated C-dots by microwave treatment of ethylenediamine and glycerol, the amino group is used for binding naphthalimide-azide by amidation reaction. Hydrogen sulfide sensor was constructed based on the reaction of naphthalimide-azide with H2S and the subsequent fluorescence change.

Owing to their outstanding physical and chemical properties, C-dots have been utilized for the construction of novel sensors for the detection of DNA, α-fetoprotein, glucose, anthrax biomarker, phosphate, peroxynitrite or metal ions (Zhao et al., 2011a, 2011b; Simões et al., 2014; Dai et al., 2012; Cui et al., 2015; Gu et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2015). But no literature reports the use of C-dots for the food safety control. As the most predominant and toxic of mycotoxins, aflatoxins are carcinogenic metabolites produced by Aspergillus flavus and Aspergillus parasiticus during the growth of crops such as corn, peanut, grain, and feed stuff. Among all 20 aflatoxin derivatives, aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) possesses the highest toxicity and is listed as group I carcinogens by the International Agency for Research on Cancer. Exposure to AFB1 may cause acute cirrhosis, necrosis and carcinoma of the liver in human beings and in animals (Li et al., 2009). Therefore, the development of a reliable analytical method for AFB1 selective sensing is essential for human health and safety.

The common methods for AFB1 detection include chromatography techniques and immunoassay. Chromatography is the most often used method for the quantification of AFB1 due to its accuracy. However, the method requires heavy instrumentation and is time-consuming, which is unsuitable for high throughput sample analysis and on-site detection (Shim et al., 2007). Immunoassay, especially the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), has been widely utilized for the determination of AFB1 in recent years. This method is highly sensitive and selective, but often requires long reaction time, complex operation process. In addition, antibodies and enzymes are easily denatured during storage which seriously limits their practical applications. A simple, rapid, and low-cost approach for AFB1 detection is thus of significant importance for the food industry (Shim et al., 2014a, 2014b).

Aptamers are DNA or RNA oligonucleotides which can specifically bind to target molecules. Aptamers possess great advantages over antibodies, such as high stability, low-cost, and easy synthesis and labeling (Famulok and Mayer, 2011). There are many aptamer based assay methods developed in recent years for the selective sensing of a variety of target molecules (Amaya-González et al., 2013; Wang and Yu, 2010). However, in comparison to ELISA, there are few aptamer-based AFB1 sensors developed. For example, Shim et al. (2014a, 2014b) developed a dipstick assay for AFB1 sensing based on a competitive reaction of AFB1 and a cy5-modified complementary strand of DNA towards aptamer. The lowest detection limit of this sensor for AFB1 is about 0.1ng/ml, demonstrating a good potential for practical applications. Lu and co-workers develop an AFB1 sensing method based on graphene quenching the fluorescence of CdTe quantum dots. The sensor displays sensitive fluorescence response due to the specific binding of AFB1 to the aptamer and the subsequent fluorescence recovery (Lu et al., 2015). These studies confirm the feasibility and potential application of an aptasensor for AFB1 detection in real samples. Fluorescence aptasensor has been widely employed in chemical and biomedical research fields due to their simple operation and high sensitivity. Compared with traditional fluorescence sensor, C-dots based aptasensor exhibits greater potential due to their superiority in chemical inertness, ease of functionalization and resistance to photobleaching (Li et al., 2012).

Mirkin et al. develop DNA sensor for the detection of mRNA in living cells based on the highly efficient fluorescence quenching properties of AuNPs (Seferos et al., 2007; Giljohann et al., 2010). The results demonstrate that AuNPs-based fluorescence sensor cause high signal and sensitivity. In this work, the AFB1 specific aptamer and a novel N,C-dots were used for the fabrication of the sensitive AuNPs-based AFB1 sensor for the first time. The AFB1 specific aptamer was covalently attached to the surface of the AuNPs. N,C-dots were assembled on aptamer modified gold nanoparticles (Aptamer/AuNPs) via electrostatic interactions. The fluorescence of the N,C-dots was quenched. Addition of AFB1 caused binding of AFB1 to the aptamer and dissociation of N,C-dots from the AuNPs. N,C-dots fluorescence recovery was observed, which could be used for the quantification of AFB1. The assay shows high sensitivity and selectivity, and can be used for AFB1 determination in real corn samples.

Oligonucleotides used in this study were synthesized and purified with PAGE by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The sequences of the oligonucleotides are as follows: AFB1 aptamer: HS-AAA AAA GTT GGG CAC GTG TTG TCT CTC TGT GTC TCG TGC CCT TCG CTA GGC CCA CA (Guo et al., 2014); A randomly selected control oligonucleotide: HS-AAA AAA TGT GGG CCT AGC GAA GGG CAC GAA TCG AGA CGT AGT.

Tris(2-carboxyethyl) phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), trisodium citrate (99%), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4, 99.9%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) was supplied by the National Standard Reference Center (Beijing, China). Other mycotoxins used in the specificity evaluation section including ochratoxin A (OTA), deoxynivalenol (DON), fumonisin B1 (FB1) and zearalenone (ZEN) were bought from Fermentek (Jerusalem, Israel). AFB2 and AFG1 were purchased from Huaan Magnech Bio-Tech Co.Ltd., Beijing. All glasswares were thoroughly cleaned with freshly prepared 3:1 HCl/HNO3 (aqua regia) and rinsed thoroughly with ultrapure water (≥18.2MΩ) prior to use. Deionized water from a Milli-Q system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, U.S.A.) was used for all solutions in this study.

UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were recorded with Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer and ShimadzuRF-5301PC spectrofluorometer, respectively. The Zeta potential measurement was carried out at 25°C (Zetasizer Nano 90, Malvern). Morphologies of the synthesized nanoparticles were characterized by Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100). In addition, the chemical composition of C-dots was characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). High performance liquid chromatograph (HPLC) consists of a vacuum degasser, a quaternary pump, an automatic sample injection system. The derivatization system was an electrochemical cell (Kobra-Cell1; R-Biopharm, Glasgow, Scotland).

N,C-dots were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of pancreatin. In brief, 2g pancreatin was dissolved in 10mL deionized water. The solution was sealed in a 25mL Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 10h. The autoclave was cooled down after the reaction to room temperature. The obtained dark brown solution was collected by centrifugation at 8000rpm for 15min to remove the precipitates. The color of the solution changed from dark brown to light brown after centrifugation. The remaining N,C-dots have an average size of 4.5nm and exhibit strong blue luminescence under UV light irradiation. The concentration of the N,C-dots was quantified by taking 1mL of the suspension and weighing after drying in a vacuum oven at 60°C for 10h.

AuNPs were synthesized according to our previous report (Wang et al., 2011). In a typical synthesis, 200mL of 0.1mM HAuCl4 solution was heated to boil under vigorous stirring, then 4mL of 38.8mM trisodium citrate solution was added rapidly. The solution color changed from pale yellow to wine red, the reaction was allowed to continue for another 30min, then cooled down to room temperature. The final AuNPs concentration was determined to be ~2.6nM by UV–vis absorption spectra based on a molar extinction coefficient of 2.7×108 M−1 cm−1 at λ=520nm (Jin et al., 2003).

Thiol-modified aptamer was attached onto the surface of the AuNPs according to a reported literature method (Zhang et al., 2012). Generally, the thiol-labeled DNA was treated with 10mM TCEP (DNA: TCEP=1:5) for 1h to cleave the disulfide bond. The activated DNA was mixed with 2.6nM AuNPs at fixed ratios, and 500mMpH 3.0 citrate-HCl buffer was added to facilitate DNA binding. After 30min incubation at room temperature with gently shaking on the shaker, the free DNA was removed by centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 25min and rinsed three times with 10mM PBS (pH, 7.0). Finally, the obtained Aptamer/AuNPs were re-dispersed in ultrapure water and stored at 4°C. The number of aptamer modified on each AuNPs was estimated by the concentration of AuNPs and aptamer. The concentration of aptamer modified on AuNPs was determined based on the decrease of absorbance at 260nm after modification.

The positively charged N,C-dots (final concentration 2.8μg/mL) were mixed with the Aptamer/AuNPs in 10mM PBS (pH 7.0). The sample solution was centrifuged to remove the excessive N,C-dots. After that, different concentrations of AFB1 (0–2.40ng/mL) were added and mixed, and the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were recorded. In order to evaluate the selectivity of the assay, six other mycotoxins (OTA, FB1, DON, ZEN, AFB2 and AFG1) were tested as possible interference substances. The final concentration of AFB1 was 2.4ng/mL, and the final concentration of the other mycotoxin was 10ng/mL. All emission spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 370nm and fluorescent data were acquired with under emission wavelength of 445nm. Quartz cuvettes with 10-mm path length were used for UV–vis and emission. All spectra were taken at room temperature in 10mM PBS at pH 7.0. All experiments data is obtained by taking the average of three replicates.

The peanut and corn samples purchased from a local supermarket were finely grounded with a laboratory mill. 4g sample was taken and mixed with 10mL methanol–water (80:20, v/v) and vigorously stirred for 5min. After centrifugation at 4000rpm for 5min, 0.5mL supernatant was taken and diluted with 1mL water. Different concentrations of AFB1 were added, and the AFB1 concentration was analyzed.

For HPLC determination, a fluorescence detector and the Chemstation software package to control the instrument, data acquisition and data analysis (Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, USA) was used for separation and determination of AFB1. Separation was carried out on a C-18 reverse phase column (Shim-pack VP-ODS C18, 5µm, 4.6mm×250mm column, Waters, USA) and the mobile phase water/methanol/acetonitrile (60:20:20 v/v). For obtaining the best wavelengths for excitation and emission of AFB1 fluorescence, the flow of mobile phase was stopped and the wavelengths (excitation and emission) were scanned with fluorescence detector. The optima for excitation and emission wavelengths were 365 and 425nm, respectively.

Gold nanoparticles are excellent quencher for fluorescence sensing due to their extraordinary high molar extinction coefficients and broad energy bandwidth (Saha et al., 2012). We employed N,C-dots as the fluorescence probe and 13nm AuNPs as the quencher for the construction of the aptasensor. AuNPs were synthesized by a typical citrate reduction method. The mercapto group labeled oligonucleotide (AFB1 aptamer) was attached onto the surface of the AuNPs. The aptamer is negatively charged. Positively charged N,C-dots could be easily assembled on the AuNPs by electrostatic interactions, and formed the C-dots/Aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposite. Simultaneously, the fluorescence of the N,C-dots was efficiently quenched by the AuNPs. With the addition of AFB1, AFB1 bound to the aptamer, N,C-dots were released, and a turn on N,C-dots fluorescence signal was detected. The emission intensity increase could be used for the quantification of the amount of AFB1 added ( Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N,C-dots and AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Novel N,C-dots were prepared by the hydrothermal treatment of pancreatin. TEM image of the N,C-dots illustrates that well dispersed carbon nanoparticles with an average diameter of 4.5nm were successfully obtained ( Fig. 1A). The inset shows the lattice spacing distance of 0.22nm, which agrees well with the 002 spacing of graphitic carbon, and is similar to other previously reported C-dots (Baker and Baker, 2010; Tetsuka et al., 2012). XPS and FTIR were used to analyze the chemical composition of the prepared N,C-dots. As shown in Fig. 1B, the XPS spectrum reveals that the N,C-dots are mainly composed of carbon (61.33%), oxygen (31.62%) and nitrogen (7.12%). The high resolution bands of C1s, O1s, and N1s demonstrate that the N,C-dots mainly contain C–C, C–O, C=O and –NH2 groups (as shown in Fig. S1, Supplementary material). The FTIR spectrum in Fig. S2 further confirms the presence of C=O, C=C, C–N, O–H, and N–H groups. In addition, Zeta-potential of the fluorescence materials was determined to be +21.20mV. These results indicate that the N,C-dots was suitable for our design.

Fig. S3 shows the UV/Vis absorption and emission spectra of the N,C-dots. The N,C-dots display strong absorption at 283nm, which correspond to the π→π* transition of the aromatic structure (Dong et al. 2013). The N,C-dots display bright blue luminescence under the UV light (365nm). The emission spectrum indicates that the maximum emission wavelength was 445nm at 370nm excitation. The fluorescence quantum yield was calculated to be 26.8% with quinine sulfate as the standard.

The AuNPs were synthesized by a common method. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) was reduced with citric acid. The thiol-labeled AFB1 aptamer was attached onto the surface of the AuNPs via the formation of the Au–S bond. The amount of the DNA aptamer modified on the AuNPs was quantified by monitoring the changes of the aptamer UV–vis absorption in the supernatant. The results show that there are about 50 aptamer molecules on each AuNP.

When the N,C-dots were added to the solution of Aptamer/AuNPs, the N,C-dots/aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposite was assembled, and meanwhile the fluorescence of the N,C-dots was effectively quenched. Compared the HRTEM image of AuNPs ( Fig. 2C) with that of the N,C-dots/aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposite (Fig. 2D), we can clearly see that the N,C-dots were distributed on the surface of the AuNPs. Fig. S4 shows that the Zeta potential of the AuNPs was −27.15mV attributed to the existence of the negatively charged citrate ions on the surface. When the negatively charged AFB1-aptamer was attached to the surface of the AuNPs, the Zeta potential value decreased to −32.30mV due to the formation of the Aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposite. By contrast, incorporation of the positively charged N,C-dots led to dramatic increase of the Zeta potential from −32.30mV to −1.70mV. In addition, XPS spectra was performed to further confirm the formation of the N,C-dots/aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. S5, evident O1s, N1s, and C1s peaks were observed. Although P and Au peaks were too low to be displayed, but the high resolution bands of the two elements can be evidently observed. These results demonstrated that the C-dots were successfully assembled onto the surface of the Aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of C-dots on fluorescence recovery
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the effect of N,C-dots on the detection, we synthesized three other N,C-dots by hydrothermal treatment of chitosan, garlic, and glycine, respectively. The obtained C-dots are denoted as N,C-dots-2, N,C-dots-3, and N,C-dots-4, respectively. The Zeta-potential values of these N,C-dots (including the N,C-dots, N,C-dots-2, N,C-dots-3, and N,C-dots-4) were determined to be +21.20, +3.21, +3.04, and −23.1mV, respectively. When the four N,C-dots were added to the solution of Aptamer/AuNPs, fluorescence quenching was observed. The quenching efficiency values were 71.8%, 38.6%, 25.9% and 44.5%, respectively (as shown in Fig. 3). In addition, HRTEM images in Fig. S6 also display the clear difference between them. For the N,C-dots/aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposite, thick N,C-dots were observed on the surface of AuNPs. For the other three nanocomposite, the corresponding N,C-dots can be found on the surface of AuNPs, but the number of nanoparticles was less than the N,C-dots. Literature reports have shown that AuNPs bind targets mainly by electrostatic force, hydrophobic interactions, or covalent Au–S or Au–N bonds. Since all the C-dots display excellent water solubility and biocompatibility (Baker and Baker, 2010), the interactions between the AuNPs and N,C-dots were attributed to mostly electrostatic interactions and chemical bonding. The surface charge of the N,C-dots will affect not only the interactions of the aptamer with the N,C-dots but also the fluorescence quenching efficiency and the sensing performance.

With the addition of 4ng/mL AFB1, specific binding of AFB1 to the nucleic acid aptamer weakened the binding between the aptamer and the N,C-dots. N,C-dots were released, and the emission of the N,C-dots recovered. The fluorescence of these N,C-dots recovered 89.5%, 50.6%, 41.8%, and 28%, respectively (Fig. 3). The results are consistent with the Zeta-potential of these N,C-dots. For the pancreatin-based N,C-dots, highly surface positive charge density resulted in most of the N,C-dots bond to the Aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposite via electrostatic interactions. Specific binding of AFB1 to the aptamer can efficiently replace the adsorbed N,C-dots. For the N,C-dots-4 prepared from glycine, the negative surface charge led to N,C-dots-4 mainly bind with Aptamer/AuNPs by chemical bonds between AuNPs and nitrogenous groups on N,C-dots-4. Because the binding was less reversible, binding of AFB1 with aptamer only caused little fluorescence recovery.

In order to get the best AFB1 sensing performance, the experimental conditions were optimized. The Aptamer/AuNPs mole ratio affects the density of the aptamer on the AuNPs, and therefore influences the interactions with the N,C-dots. Different amounts of the aptamer were added to the solution of the AuNPs, and different Aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposites were prepared. Fig. S7A shows the changes in fluorescence quenching of the N,C-dots upon the addition of different Aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposites and the percentage of recovery in the presence of AFB1. When a ratio of 100:1 (Aptamer:AuNPs) was used, the Aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposite displayed high quenching efficiency but the fluorescence recovery was insufficient. It seems that since the amount of the DNA aptamer attached to the AuNPs was limited, the N,C-dots could covalently connect to the surface of the AuNPs. When AFB1 was added, it could not replace the N,C-dots, and thus less efficient fluorescence recovery was observed. By contrast, too much aptamer attached to the AuNPs (Aptamer/AuNPs=300:1) would lead to insufficient binding of the aptamer/AuNPs to the N,C-dots, and the quenching efficiency was not satisfactory. The best fluorescence quenching and recovery were obtained when the aptamer/AuNPs ratio was 200:1.

In addition, the assay buffer pH, ionic strength and incubation time were also optimized. When the buffer pH value increased from 3.0 to 7.0, the fluorescence recovery increased gradually (Fig. S7B). Further increase of the assay pH values to 8.0 and 9.0 caused significant decrease in assay performance. The concentration of NaCl did not show obvious influence on the fluorescence recovery at a concentration of less than 50mM (Fig. S7C). The aptasensor shows rapid and stable response to AFB1. As shown in Fig. S7D, the recovered fluorescence signal shows not obvious changes after 5min of incubation, indicating that the interactions between AFB1 and the N,C-dots/Aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposite was rapid, and the equilibrium was reached in 5min.

Therefore, for the fabrication of the AFB1 sensor, an Aptamer/AuNPs ratio of 200:1 was selected for modification of the AuNPs. AFB1 detection was carried out in 10mM pH 7.0 PBS buffer solution containing 10mM NaCl and with an incubation time of 5min.

The quantification of AFB1 was carried out using the optimized assay conditions. Firstly, excessive N,C-dots were mixed with Aptamer/AuNPs and incubated for 5min. The free N,C-dots were removed via centrifugation, the precipitate was dispersed in 10mM PBS (pH, 7.0). Different concentrations of AFB1 were added and the emission intensity changes were recorded ( Fig. 4A). The results clearly show that with the addition of increasing concentrations of AFB1, a gradually increased turn-on fluorescence signal was observed. A linear fluorescence signal response to AFB1 concentration was obtained over a wide AFB1 concentration range of 0.005–2.00ng/mL. The linear regression equation is:
F

/

= 0.549
C
A

1
+ 1.089 , (
R
2
= 0.996 )

F 2 refers to the recovered fluorescence intensity, F 1 is the background fluorescence signal, C AFB1 is the concentration of AFB1 in ng/mL. The assay is highly sensitive, a detection limit of AFB1 was calculated to be 5pg/mL (16pM, S/N=3). Our assay shows better detection sensitivity without using amplification techniques than most of recently reported immunoassay and electrochemical methods (Table S1, Supplementary material). In addition, the detection process takes only about 5min. This allows rapid detection of a large number of samples in a short period of time, which is an essential requirement for food quality control.

Selectivity of the aptasensor was investigated. OTA, ZEN, FB1, DON, AFB2 and AFG1 normally coexist with AFB1 in mildewed agriculture products. As shown in Fig. 5A and Fig. S8, the N,C-dots/Aptamer/AuNPs based aptasensor provides high selectivity against the other mycotoxins. The selectivity apparently originated from the high selectivity of the AFB1-aptamer.

A thiol-labeled randomly selected oligonucleotide (r-DNA) was attached to the AuNPs. However, when 5ng/mL AFB1 was added to the sample solution of the N,C-dots/r-DNA/AuNPs nanocomposite, little N,C-dots fluorescence recovery was observed (Fig. 5B). Direct mixing of N,C-dots with the AuNPs led to evident fluorescence quenching, but no fluorescence recovery could be observed in the presence of AFB1. In addition, simple mixing of N,C-dots with the aptamer or AFB1 show little effect on the emission of the N,C-dots (Fig. S9). These results clearly confirm that the selectivity of the aptasensor is attributed to the specific binding of AFB1 to the nucleic acid aptamer and the aptasensor is very selective against AFB1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AFB1 in the peanut and corn samples
ce:section.ce:para: Peanut and corn samples were purchased from a local supermarket and prepared according to the method described in Section 2.6. The samples were spiked with different concentrations of AFB1. The results are summarized in Table S2. The average recovery values are in the range of 92.00–105.00%, and the RSD values are lower than 3%. The results clearly indicate that our assay can be used for the detection of AFB1 detection in real samples. To further verify the feasibility of the proposed method, the same samples were determined by HPLC (as shown in Table S2 and Figs. S10 and S11). The results indicate that the proposed method is more sensitive than HPLC in low concentration due to the high background interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of the proposed method for the detection of OTA
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify whether this aptamer/AuNPs/N,C-dots structure can be applied to detect other targets with specific aptamers, we attempted to modify AuNPs with the aptamer of OTA and investigate the detection of OTA with this OTA-aptamer/AuNPs/N,C-dots biosensor. Under the same experimental conditions of AFB1, the OTA-aptamer/AuNPs/N,C-dots were prepared and utilized in OTA detection. As shown in Fig. 6, the sensor shows sensitive response towards OTA, and the fluorescence signal is directly proportional to the concentration of OTA in the concentration range of 4.20×10−3–3.00ng/mL. The detection limit was calculated to be about 4.2pg/mL (S/N=3). These results demonstrated that this proposed method could be applied for other targets by changing corresponding aptamer.

In conclusion, an ultrasensitive aptamer-based biosensor for the detection of AFB1 is developed. Positively charged fluorescent N,C-dots were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of pancreatin. The N,C-dots show excellent photophysical properties with a fluorescence quantum yield of 26.8%. The N,C-dots were assembled to the aptamer/AuNPs and formed the N,C-dots/aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposite, which resulted in N,C-dots fluorescence quenching. The specific interactions between AFB1 and the aptamer caused release of the N,C-dots and fluorescence recovery. The intensity of the recovered fluorescence was found to be proportional to the concentration of the added AFB1. A fairly simple, rapidly respond, and low cost aptasensor was developed. The sensor is highly sensitive and selective, and could be used for the detection of AFB1 in real samples. In addition, we demonstrated that the proposed method could be used for other target assay by replacing the corresponding aptamer, suggesting that the assay strategy may be used for the development of other aptamer based highly efficient sensors in other fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Basic Research Project (973 Program, Nos. 2013CB127804, 2011CB911002), PhD start-up grants SWU113112, SWU113111 from Southwest University, and ACBM 2014004 from Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), Wuhan University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.bios.2015.11.015.
